payload.answer: Store the CSV content as a structured dataset reference in external/tabular storage with indexing on id; keep only durable schema/metadata and row-lookup/deduplication rules in Brain. Do not store all 500 rows as individual durable memory facts.
payload.decision: Store as a structured dataset reference (external/tabular storage) rather than as individual durable memory items; persist only the schema and key indexing/deduplication rules in Brain's durable memory, and keep row-level data in a scalable table store with retrieval-on-demand.

entity_resolution.reason: No unique stable entity identifier for the dataset/table itself was provided beyond the fixture label; avoid creating a durable entity id without explicit guidance.

payload.intent: Determine an appropriate storage strategy for a large CSV table (500 rows) with repeated fields (id, name, value).

memory_cards.statement: For large, repetitive tabular data (e.g., 500+ CSV rows), do not encode every row as separate durable memory facts; instead store the table in a dedicated tabular datastore and retain only schema/metadata and retrieval rules as durable memory.

Use the table's primary key field (e.g., id) as the identifier for row lookups; if duplicates exist, apply a deterministic deduplication strategy (e.g., keep the latest row by an ordering rule) or store multiple values per id when appropriate.

Persist in-memory/durable records only what is required to answer queries over the table (e.g., schema, allowed filters, and index strategy); fetch specific rows from the tabular datastore at query time based on constraints (id/name/value).

details: Committed storage strategy favoring external structured storage for large tabular data.
Avoided creating a specific dataset entity id due to missing explicit identifier.

repair_options: If id is not unique, switch from single-row-per-id storage to a one-to-many model (id -> list of (name,value) pairs) in the datastore.
If the system requires durable recall of specific rows, store a small, curated subset of rows as durable memory cards and keep the full table elsewhere for bulk retrieval.
If deduplication criteria are defined (e.g., 'latest' timestamp), encode that deterministic rule in durable memory metadata.
